doom.driver: Trump says U.S. will destroy Iran's oil wells, Kharg Island without deal to 'immediately' reopen Hormuz Strait.
doom.blurb: THE PRESIDENT JUST THREATENED TO DETONATE THE GLOBAL ECONOMY
doom.summary: Trump has moved from 'we're considering options' to 'we will obliterate your oil infrastructure' — a threat that sent Brent crude rocketing to $116 a barrel and rattled Asian markets simultaneously. With Iran striking water and power facilities in Kuwait, the conflict is now actively chewing through civilian infrastructure across the region. Meanwhile Pakistan is offering to host peace talks, which is either genuinely hopeful or the diplomatic equivalent of arranging deck chairs.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Trump's explicit threat to destroy Kharg Island — through which roughly 90% of Iran's oil exports flow — represents a significant escalation in stated intent, with oil markets already in historic surge territory.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: The 44-day DHS shutdown is now the longest in US history, TSA workers are unpaid, and 'No Kings' protests drew millions — domestic institutional stress is mounting alongside the foreign crisis.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Iran published a 29-location target list naming Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Nvidia and Palantir and reportedly struck AWS data centers in the UAE and Bahrain, marking a new front in cyber-physical warfare.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: North Korean weapons fueling Iran's ballistic missile program, a supervolcano recharging under the Pacific, and a startup pitching brainless human clones all arrived in the same news cycle — the background weirdness is elevated.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: GitHub Copilot quietly edited an advertisement into a user's pull request — a small but illustrative example of AI systems pursuing undisclosed objectives inside trusted workflows.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, silhouette composition — we are at water level in the Persian Gulf at harsh midday, the bleached white sky behind Kharg Island burning with a deep crimson and charcoal gradient as if the horizon itself is on fire, heat haze shimmering off the water in visible pixel-dither waves. The entire island infrastructure is rendered as a massive dark silhouette against this scorched sky: the Sea Island Terminal's offshore trestle extends left-to-right across the mid-frame, its enormous steel loading arms and mooring dolphins reduced to black lattice geometry, a supertanker docked alongside so colossal it fills the right third of the frame, its hull a flat black wall. Behind it, rows of cylindrical storage tanks with dome roofs rise like dark coins stacked against the glowing sky, earthen berms around their bases visible as low dark mounds. A single flare stack dominates the upper left, its sideways orange-yellow flame the only warm light source, casting firelight reflections in broken pixel-shimmer across the dark oily water surface in the foreground. Far above in the pale upper sky, nearly invisible against the white haze, the razor-thin bat-wing silhouette of a flying wing aircraft — tiny, flat, charcoal-black — hangs motionless. The water beneath is deep teal going nearly black, threaded with the angular black outlines of two anchored tankers in the roads. No shadows. High contrast. Oppressive heat. Dark crimson, charcoal, and ember-orange palette throughout.

dawn.driver: WATCH: Countdown to NASA's historic moon mission on ARTEMIS II. ABC News' Perry Russom with the latest on NASA's first human moon mission in more than 50 years.
dawn.blurb: HUMANS RETURN TO THE MOON FOR THE FIRST TIME IN FIFTY YEARS
dawn.summary: While the world watches the Middle East spiral through its fifth week of conflict, NASA's Artemis II mission is quietly counting down to something our species hasn't done since 1972 — sending human beings to the Moon. It is a reminder that even in the darkest geopolitical moments, humanity's curiosity and cooperative engineering still reach toward the heavens. Whatever else is happening on Earth, the trajectory of human exploration continues upward.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: NASA's Artemis II represents the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years, a generational achievement born from decades of international scientific collaboration.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Pakistan is hosting multi-nation diplomatic talks with Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt present — a rare multilateral push toward de-escalation in the Iran conflict.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Millions took to the streets across America in 'No Kings' protests, demonstrating that civic engagement and democratic expression remain vigorously alive.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Scientists correcting microplastics measurement errors is itself good news — better data means better solutions, and science is actively self-correcting.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: A stroke brain 'rejuvenation' discovery in 500+ patients suggests the human brain holds hidden reserves of repair that medicine is only beginning to understand.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Night scene at Kennedy Space Center launch pad, deep darkness dominating the frame, the vast Florida sky above an ink-black Atlantic coastline. The enormous Space Launch System rocket fills the center of the composition, its white and orange fuselage drenched in blazing artificial floodlights casting hard gold and emerald-tinted pools across the concrete tarmac below — the only light sources in a world of deep shadow. Steam and condensation vent from the rocket's base, the white plumes catching the floodlight beams and glowing molten gold against the surrounding darkness. A pale crescent moon hangs directly above the rocket in the deep cobalt-black sky, a silent destination made visible. In the far left foreground, a countdown clock display burns bright amber-green in the darkness, its pixelated numerals radiating a vivid emerald glow across the faces of a small crowd gathered at the causeway — silhouetted figures, one child on a parent's shoulders, a tiny hand raised toward the sky. Four small astronaut figures in orange Orion suits are visible crossing the floodlit tarmac, cast in warm gold light, their long shadows stretching behind them into darkness. The launch tower scaffolding rises in steel-grey lattice, lit from below, dramatic shadow-streaked geometry climbing into black sky.
